name: MVP / Incremental Delivery
year_developed: 2001 (Frank Robinson coined 'Minimum Viable Product'); 2011 (Eric Ries redefined and popularized MVP in 'The Lean Startup', building on Steve Blank's Customer Development methodology from ~2003-2005); incremental delivery concept older — rooted in iterative development (Larman & Basili, 1970s-1990s) and military 'sequels' concept (planned follow-on phases in campaign planning)
progenitor: Frank Robinson (coined MVP term, 2001, defining it as the product with maximum ROI divided by risk); Eric Ries (popularized and redefined MVP in The Lean Startup, 2011, as the version that enables maximum validated learning with least effort); Steve Blank (Customer Development methodology, The Four Steps to the Epiphany, 2005 — foundation for Lean Startup); the concept also draws from John Boyd's OODA loop (military strategy, 1960s), lean manufacturing (Toyota Production System, Ohno, 1950s), and Alistair Cockburn's walking skeleton concept
driving_insight: Building a complete product before exposing it to customers is the single largest source of startup failure — you invest months/years building something nobody wants. The MVP inverts this: ship the smallest thing that generates validated learning about customer value, then iterate. The insight is epistemological, not technical: your beliefs about what customers want are hypotheses to be tested, not requirements to be fulfilled. Build-Measure-Learn is a scientific method for product development: each increment is an experiment. The military 'sequels' concept (campaigns planned as a series of follow-on phases with each phase's feasibility dependent on previous phase outcomes) is the same structural insight in a different domain — you cannot plan Phase 3 in detail until you see the results of Phase 1.
decision_style: deliberative and evidence-driven — each increment delivers product AND data. The data (customer behavior, usage metrics, feedback) informs the decision about what to build next: persevere (continue on the current path), pivot (change strategy while preserving learnings), or kill (abandon the initiative). Decisions are made by the product team with stakeholder input, guided by the evidence from the current increment.
problem_class_targeted: ill-defined, high-uncertainty product development problems where customer needs are NOT fully known upfront and the risk of building the wrong product is high. Specifically targeted at startups (extreme uncertainty, limited resources) and new product initiatives within established companies. The incremental delivery aspect also applies to well-defined problems where risk reduction through early partial delivery is valuable (e.g., infrastructure projects, military deployments). Less suited to: safety-critical systems where partial delivery is unacceptable (pacemaker firmware), regulatory one-shot deployments (tax system overhaul), or commoditized products where customer learning offers no advantage.
ordered_phases: (1) Identify Hypotheses: list the riskiest assumptions about customer needs, value proposition, and business model; (2) Define MVP: design the smallest experiment that tests the riskiest hypothesis — this may be a working product, a landing page, a concierge service, a video, or a prototype; (3) Build: create the MVP with the minimum effort required to generate learning; (4) Measure: collect data from real customer interactions with the MVP (usage analytics, conversion rates, qualitative feedback); (5) Learn: analyze data — was the hypothesis validated or invalidated? What new insights emerged?; (6) Decide: persevere (keep building with refined understanding), pivot (change strategy while preserving learnings), or kill (abandon); (7) Increment: each cycle produces a more complete product, a richer understanding of the customer, and a more validated business model. Rinse and repeat.
feedback_loops: The Build-Measure-Learn loop IS the feedback loop — it is the central mechanism, not an afterthought. The entire methodology is structured around rapid cycling through this loop. The loop is explicit and designed (prescribed cadence: as fast as possible, typically 1-4 weeks per cycle). Feedback comes from: customer usage data (quantitative), customer interviews (qualitative), A/B test results, funnel analytics, and churn/satisfaction metrics. The loop directly informs both product direction (what features to add/remove) and strategy (persevere/pivot/kill).
gates: The primary gate is the 'persevere/pivot/kill' decision at the end of each Build-Measure-Learn cycle. This is a formal decision point: the team presents learnings and a recommendation to stakeholders (or makes the decision autonomously if empowered). Gate criteria: has the hypothesis been validated (metrics met the predetermined threshold)? If not, is pivoting likely to find a better path, or should the initiative be killed? A secondary gate is the MVP definition itself — the MVP must be truly minimal (larger-than-minimal = wasted learning time).
exit_criteria: The incremental delivery process is never 'done' in the traditional sense — the product is continuously improved as long as it delivers value. However, a phase is 'done' when: the hypothesis has been tested, the data has been analyzed, and a persevere/pivot/kill decision has been made. The product itself reaches a 'done for now' state when the next cycle of learning has been planned. Partial-completion states: a partially-failed experiment (ambiguous data) is still valuable learning but delays the decision.
decomposition_method: value-driven, increment-driven. The product is decomposed NOT by technical architecture (database, API, UI) but by value-delivery increments — each increment is a complete, usable, end-to-end slice of the product that delivers value to a subset of customers for a subset of use cases. The first increment (MVP) is the smallest slice that can generate meaningful learning. Subsequent increments add value slices (more features, more customer segments, more use cases) in priority order. This is fundamentally a vertical decomposition (slice through all layers) rather than horizontal (build all of one layer first).
granularity_guidance: MVP granularity: the smallest thing that can generate VALIDATED LEARNING about the riskiest hypothesis. Ries emphasizes that an MVP is NOT necessarily a product — it can be: a landing page (Dropbox's explainer video MVP), a concierge service (manual behind-the-scenes fulfillment), a Wizard of Oz prototype (fake back-end, real front-end), or a single-feature product. Increment granularity: each increment should be shippable in 1-4 weeks (typical lean startup cycle). The granularity heuristic is: what's the fastest cycle that still produces statistically meaningful learning? For consumer products with large user bases, this can be days; for enterprise products, weeks.
boundary_rules: Each increment must be a complete vertical slice — it must work end-to-end for the target use case without relying on future increments. Boundaries between increments are value boundaries (increment N delivers value proposition A; increment N+1 adds value proposition B) rather than technical boundaries. The boundary rule is: NO orphaned increments — you ship nothing that doesn't deliver a complete, usable user outcome. This prevents the 'integration hell' of phased technical delivery where components don't integrate until the final phase.
risk_assessment_mechanism: The entire methodology IS a risk assessment mechanism. Risk is prioritized by hypothesis criticality: the riskiest assumption is tested first. If the riskiest hypothesis is invalidated early, the venture can pivot or die before wasting resources on lower-risk assumptions. The Build-Measure-Learn loop continuously re-assesses risk: validated learning reduces uncertainty; invalidated hypotheses surface new risks. No formal risk scoring — risk is qualitatively ranked by: 'if this assumption is wrong, does the entire venture fail?'
unknown_handling: Unknowns are the fuel of the methodology — the entire process is designed to convert unknowns into knowns (validated learning). An unknown customer need translates into a hypothesis to be tested in the next increment. If an unknown cannot be tested (e.g., regulatory approval outcome that takes 2 years), it is acknowledged as a 'known unknown' and factored into the pivot/kill decision criteria. The methodology explicitly rejects resolving unknowns through analysis or expert opinion — resolution must come through empirical testing.
default_stance_on_unknown: Neither purely permissive nor restrictive — the stance is EMPIRICAL. Do not assume the unknown is safe (build anyway) or dangerous (halt); instead, design an experiment to resolve the unknown. When an experiment is infeasible (cannot test a 2-year regulatory process in a 2-week cycle), the default is RESTRICTIVE — flag the risk and make a conscious decision about whether to proceed with unresolved uncertainty.
safety_guardrails: None for operational safety. MVP/Incremental Delivery is a product development methodology, not a safety framework. In safety-critical domains (medical devices, aviation, autonomous vehicles), incremental delivery must be supplemented with formal safety validation (FDA 510(k), DO-178C, ISO 26262). However, the methodology can be applied to safety systems IF each increment passes independent safety gates before customer exposure. Ries explicitly warns against applying lean startup methods to safety-critical decisions — validated learning cannot justify endangering customers.
human_decision_points: Humans are involved at: (1) hypothesis formulation — what to test; (2) MVP design — what constitutes a valid experiment; (3) data interpretation — what does the data mean (statistical significance vs. practical significance); (4) persevere/pivot/kill decision — the highest-stakes judgment call; (5) increment scope definition — what goes in the next slice. The human is IN the loop for all strategic decisions. The Build-Measure phases can be partially automated (A/B testing platforms, analytics pipelines), but the Learn-Decide phases require human judgment.
override_capability: Complete human override: leadership can overrule the Build-Measure-Learn evidence at any point. Common overrides include: strategic pivot not supported by data (Steve Jobs's 'customers don't know what they want'), regulatory requirement (must build X regardless of customer feedback), or competitive pressure (must match competitor's feature even though it doesn't test well). Override is a judgment call; the methodology's value is making the override explicit — you're choosing to ignore evidence, not pretending the evidence says something else.
fallback_when_no_human: Not applicable. The Learn-Decide phase requires human judgment to interpret data and make strategic decisions. Automated A/B testing platforms can run the Build-Measure cycle without humans (auto-ramp the winning variant) for optimization decisions, but this is optimization within a known strategy, not strategy discovery. For novel product development, human judgment is irreplaceable.
max_problem_size: The methodology has been applied to products serving 1B+ users (Facebook's 'move fast and break things' culture is a lean startup derivative). The largest practical limit: the Build-Measure-Learn loop slows down as product complexity increases — a mobile app can cycle in 1-2 weeks; an enterprise SaaS platform may need 4-8 weeks per cycle; a hardware product may need 3-6 months. What breaks at scale: (1) cycle time increases because more stakeholders and more integration points slow down the Build phase; (2) measurement becomes noisier as user base diversifies; (3) pivots become more expensive (hard to pivot a battleship). The methodology is most effective at small-to-medium scale and requires adaptation for very large products/companies.
parallel_execution: Supports parallel execution across independent value streams: multiple MVPs can be tested simultaneously against different hypotheses. Within a single product, parallel development teams can work on different increments (one team builds increment for user segment A, another for segment B), with coordination at the integration level. However, the methodology emphasizes focused learning — testing too many hypotheses in parallel confounds the data and makes it harder to attribute outcomes to specific changes. Lean startup practitioners typically recommend limited parallelism (1-3 concurrent experiments).
team_scaling_model: Optimal for small, cross-functional teams (5-10 people) that can own a complete Build-Measure-Learn cycle. Scaling is achieved by adding autonomous teams (squads) that each own separate value streams or customer segments, following the Spotify model. Coordination overhead grows with the number of teams sharing the same codebase and user base — above ~8-10 teams, a centralized coordination layer (release train) becomes necessary, which slows down the cycle. Scaling is sub-linear: each additional team adds incrementally less learning throughput due to coordination costs.
cost_scaling_model: The cost of each Build-Measure-Learn cycle is: Build (team salary * cycle duration) + Measure (analytics infrastructure) + Learn (team analysis time). The cost per cycle is designed to be AS LOW AS POSSIBLE — the entire point of MVP is to minimize the cost of learning. Cycle cost is relatively stable across cycles (same team, same duration), but the VALUE of learning per cycle decreases over time (early learning about the riskiest assumptions is more valuable than later learning about edge cases). Total cost = cost_per_cycle * number_of_cycles. Key cost insight: the most expensive MVP cycles are the ones that take too long (wasted salary) or test the wrong thing (wasted learning).
replanning_built_in: Replanning IS the methodology — every Build-Measure-Learn cycle is a replanning event. Based on what was learned, the team decides to persevere (continue with refined plan), pivot (change the plan), or kill (abandon the plan). Replanning is first-class, frequent (every 1-4 weeks), and the primary mechanism of progress. It is always global in scope: a pivot reorients the entire product strategy, not just one feature. However, smaller course corrections (changing a feature's design based on feedback) are local replanning within the persevere branch.
trigger_mechanism: Primarily calendar-driven (end of Build-Measure-Learn cycle, typically 1-4 weeks). Also data-driven: hypothesis validated (triggers persevere/expand), hypothesis invalidated (triggers pivot/kill discussion), or unexpected data (anomalous usage pattern triggers new hypothesis formation). Crisis-driven: competitive entry, funding change, or market shift can trigger emergency pivot outside the regular cycle cadence.
adaptation_cost: Persevere: low cost — the new increment adds to existing momentum, no work is lost (previous increment becomes part of the growing product). Pivot: medium cost — some built functionality may be discarded or repurposed, but the learning (the most valuable asset) is preserved. Kill: the cost is the total investment to date (sunk cost), but early killing limits cost compared to continuing a failing initiative. The methodology explicitly treats sunk cost as irrelevant to future decisions — the only question is whether future investment is justified by expected future learning/returns.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Failure is expected and designed for: an invalidated hypothesis is a 'successful failure' — it generated learning. Recovery mechanism: (1) pivot to a new hypothesis informed by the failure data; (2) if no viable pivot exists, kill the initiative and redeploy the team. Blast radius containment: the small increment size ensures that any single failure is cheap and fast. The methodology's key recovery mechanism is the pivot — failing doesn't mean losing; it means redirecting effort based on what you learned about why you failed.
output_artifacts: Working product increments (each a shippable, tested release), validated learning documentation (hypothesis log: what we believed, what we tested, what we learned), pivot/persevere/kill decision records, customer interview transcripts and analytics dashboards, updated business model canvas (Strategyzer's Business Model Canvas is the standard companion tool), innovation accounting metrics (actionable metrics vs. vanity metrics).
briefing_format: The primary briefing format is the Build-Measure-Learn loop narrative: 'We believed [hypothesis]. We built [MVP description]. We measured [key metric] and observed [result]. Therefore we learned [insight] and decided to [persevere/pivot/kill].' For stakeholders: Lean Canvas or Business Model Canvas showing current validated state (what's proven, what's still hypothetical), cohort analysis charts showing metric trends across increments, and the prioritized hypothesis backlog for future cycles.
traceability: Strong traceability when practiced rigorously: each product feature traces back to the hypothesis it was built to test and the learning cycle that produced it. The innovation accounting framework provides a numerical audit trail (baseline, then successive improvements). However, in practice, many teams skip rigorous hypothesis documentation and move fast without recording decisions. The methodology's traceability is only as good as the team's discipline in documenting hypotheses and results.
agent_communication_protocol: Not applicable directly. The Build-Measure-Learn cycle can be represented as a state machine (Build -> Measure -> Learn -> Decide -> Build...) and communicated between orchestration agents as status updates. In a JORDAN pipeline, the plan node could output a set of hypotheses to test; the execute node builds and ships the MVP; the synthesize node measures and analyzes results; the replan node decides persevere/pivot/kill. The protocol would be custom JSON serialization of hypothesis states and experiment results.
learning_memory_architecture: The methodology IS a learning architecture: (1) hypothesis backlog = what we need to learn; (2) validated learning = what we have learned (episodic memory — specific experiment outcomes); (3) business model canvas = current synthesized understanding (semantic memory — the business model); (4) company strategy = accumulated procedural knowledge (how we operate). The methodology doesn't prescribe a specific persistent storage format; in practice, teams use wikis, experiment databases, and analytics platforms. The key memory principle: learning must be externalized (documented, shared) to persist beyond individual team members' tenure.
latency_bounds: The cycle is designed for rapid iteration: 1-4 weeks is the recommended cadence for software products. Enterprise/large-scale: 4-8 weeks. Hardware: 3-6 months. The methodology explicitly prioritizes speed of learning over build quality — a 'good enough to learn from' product is preferred to a 'perfect but slow to build' product. Under severe time pressure, the cycle compresses: smaller MVP, coarser measurement, faster decision. The methodology degrades gracefully — a longer cycle still works, just produces learning more slowly.
evaluation_benchmarking: No formal benchmarks or controlled studies. The Lean Startup methodology's validation is primarily through practitioner adoption and case studies: Ries's book has sold over 1 million copies; the methodology is taught in every major business school (Stanford, Harvard, MIT, INSEAD); startups funded by Y Combinator, Techstars, and 500 Startups are explicitly trained in lean startup methods. Ries himself acknowledges the lack of rigorous evidence and has advocated for more systematic validation. Post-hoc analyses suggest lean startup companies raise less capital before product-market fit, but correlation vs. causation is debated. The methodology's strongest validation is its convergence with military OODA loop theory from a completely independent domain.
mapping_strength: Strong direct mapping to JORDAN agent development: the JORDAN pipeline can be deployed incrementally — MVP is a minimal agent that completes simple tasks end-to-end; each subsequent increment adds capability (more tools, better reasoning, richer output). The Build-Measure-Learn loop maps to: (Build) implement a new agent capability, (Measure) evaluate it on benchmark tasks, (Learn) analyze failure modes, (Decide) improve or pivot strategy. The persevere/pivot/kill decision framework applies directly to agent development: persevere = iterate on prompt engineering; pivot = switch from CoT to ReAct architecture; kill = abandon an unpromising agent approach after empirical results show it cannot reach required performance.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN/agent contexts: (1) the methodology assumes customer feedback as the primary learning signal, but agent performance is measured against task benchmarks, not customer satisfaction — translation needed; (2) the 1-4 week cycle assumes human-speed development, but agent iterations can be much faster (automated evaluation + code generation could cycle in hours); (3) lean startup was designed for startups with extreme uncertainty; JORDAN may operate in more structured domains where the value of 'pivoting' is lower and the cost of systematic plan-then-execute is justified; (4) the methodology's emphasis on external customer feedback doesn't directly apply to internal infrastructure agents (who is the 'customer' for a research agent?); (5) MVP in agent context is tricky — a 'minimal' agent that produces dangerously wrong output is worse than no agent at all.
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) Replace 'customer feedback' with 'benchmark performance and failure mode analysis' as the learning signal — each agent increment is evaluated against a test suite; (2) accelerate the Build-Measure-Learn cycle to hours instead of weeks by automating the Build phase (code generation) and the Measure phase (automated evaluation); (3) define agent MVP as the minimal end-to-end pipeline that completes at least one task class with acceptable accuracy — a 'walking skeleton' agent; (4) use the persevere/pivot/kill framework for agent architecture decisions: persevere = optimize current approach, pivot = restructure the pipeline (e.g., add a verification node), kill = abandon a sub-agent that consistently underperforms; (5) apply the 'sequels' concept — plan the fully-capable agent as a series of successively more capable increments, each building on validated capabilities from the previous increment.
validation_context: Validated extensively through startup ecosystem adoption since 2011: Y Combinator (3000+ startups trained in lean methods), Techstars, 500 Startups, and corporate innovation labs (GE, Intuit, Adobe, Toyota). Eric Ries's The Lean Startup has sold over 1 million copies in 30+ languages. Steve Blank's Lean LaunchPad curriculum is used at NSF I-Corps (National Science Foundation Innovation Corps) to train thousands of scientists and engineers in customer discovery — providing quasi-academic validation. Military validation: John Boyd's OODA loop (the conceptual parent of Build-Measure-Learn) was validated through US Air Force fighter pilot training and adopted across NATO military doctrine.
known_failures: Documented failures: (1) MVP confusion — teams interpret MVP as 'the worst product we can get away with' rather than 'the smallest experiment that generates learning,' releasing buggy, unusable products that damage brand and drive customers away; (2) premature scaling — a validated MVP produces positive early data, the team interprets this as 'we've found product-market fit' and scales prematurely, only to discover the early adopters were not representative of the mainstream market (the chasm in Geoffrey Moore's Crossing the Chasm); (3) pivoting to nowhere — teams pivot repeatedly without ever converging, burning runway on a series of unvalidated MVPs without building cumulative learning ('pivot fatigue'); (4) measurement theater — teams collect extensive analytics but don't tie metrics to hypotheses, creating the appearance of data-drivenness without actual learning; (5) the 'genius vision' exception — some of the most successful products (iPhone, Tesla Model S, original Macintosh) were NOT developed via Lean Startup methods but through a singular visionary's conviction — Lean Startup methods would likely have killed these products because customers couldn't articulate desire for something they'd never experienced; (6) survivor bias — the startups that succeeded using Lean Startup methods are visible; the ones that failed using the same methods are invisible, so the methodology's true success rate is unknown.
where_still_used: Universally adopted in the startup ecosystem: Y Combinator, Techstars, 500 Startups portfolio companies; corporate innovation labs (Google X, Microsoft Garage, Adobe Kickbox); NSF I-Corps program (training thousands of scientists in customer discovery); business school curricula (Stanford Lean LaunchPad, Harvard i-lab, MIT delta v). Agile/Scrum teams in established companies routinely practice incremental delivery — while not always labeled 'MVP,' the practice of shipping small, complete increments is industry-standard in modern software development. The Lean Startup Conference (annual, since 2012) continues to attract thousands of attendees.
